content.title: Lebesgue Outer Measure
content.content:
For any $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, the **Lebesgue outer measure** 
of $A$ is
\[
m^*(A) = \inf\left\{\sum_{n=1}^\infty |I_n| : A \subseteq \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty I_n\right\}
\]
where the infimum is taken over all countable covers of $A$ by open intervals.

content.title: Properties of Outer Measure
content.content:
1. $m^*(\emptyset) = 0$.

2. **Monotonicity:** $A \subseteq B \implies m^*(A) \leq m^*(B)$.

3. **Countable subadditivity:** $m^*\left(\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty A_n\right) \leq \sum_{n=1}^\infty m^*(A_n)$.

4. **Length of intervals:** $m^*(I) = |I|$ for any interval $I$.

5. **Translation invariance:** $m^*(A + t) = m^*(A)$ for any $t \in \mathbb{R}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For an interval $[a, b]$:

$m^*([a,b]) \leq b - a$ since $\{(a - \varepsilon, b + \varepsilon)\}$ 
covers $[a, b]$ with total length $b - a + 2\varepsilon$.

For the reverse inequality, suppose $[a, b] \subseteq \bigcup I_n$ where 
$I_n = (a_n, b_n)$. By compactness, finitely many intervals cover $[a, b]$, 
say $I_1, \ldots, I_N$. A careful analysis shows $\sum_{n=1}^N (b_n - a_n) \geq b - a$.

content.title: Sets of Outer Measure Zero
content.content:
A set $A$ has $m^*(A) = 0$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon > 0$, 
there exists a countable cover $\{I_n\}$ with $\sum |I_n| < \varepsilon$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Countable Sets Have Measure Zero
content.problem:
If $A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots\}$ is countable, cover each $a_n$ by an 
interval of length $\varepsilon/2^n$. Then $\sum |I_n| = \varepsilon$.

content.title: The Cantor Set Has Measure Zero
content.problem:
The Cantor set $C$ is constructed by removing middle thirds. At stage $n$, 
we remove $2^{n-1}$ intervals of length $1/3^n$, for a total removed 
length of $\sum_{n=1}^\infty 2^{n-1}/3^n = 1$. Thus $m^*(C) = 0$.

content.title: Uncountable Sets Can Have Measure Zero
content.content:
The Cantor set is uncountable (it has the cardinality of $\mathbb{R}$) 
yet has measure zero. Size in the sense of cardinality and size in the 
sense of measure are different concepts.
